image_prompt: Deep limestone wine cellar beneath a 17th-century French chateau in Saint-Emilion. A long vaulted stone tunnel carved by monks, rough-hewn pale limestone walls and arched ceiling. Iron sconces along the walls holding lit candles that throw pools of warm light and long shadows. Oak wine barrels stacked floor to ceiling along one wall. A long dark wooden table in the center with a chandelier of mismatched dripping candles above it, wax stalactites dripping onto the stone floor. Bottles of red wine and crystal glasses on the table. A heavy iron-banded door at the far end. Empty, no people. Colors: warm pale limestone cream, deep candle-flame amber, rich burgundy red, black shadow. Gothic chiaroscuro atmosphere.
name: The Cellar
location: A wine cellar beneath a 17th-century chateau in Saint-Emilion, France. Three hundred feet of limestone tunnels carved by monks who needed somewhere dark and cool. The client inherited the estate and converted the deepest chamber into a private salon. The wine is not for sale.
time: 9 PM, Saturday. The dinner upstairs ended an hour ago. The staff have gone home. The cellar belongs to whoever has the key.
weather: Warm summer night above. Down here it is 55 degrees year round. The limestone keeps its own climate. Seasons are irrelevant.

atmosphere.lighting: Candles in iron sconces along the tunnel walls, spaced far enough apart that you walk through pools of light and pools of dark. A chandelier of mismatched candles over the central table, wax dripping onto the stone floor in slow stalactites. No electricity. The monks did not wire the deep chambers and the client never bothered.
atmosphere.sound: Absolute silence except for voices and the occasional drip of water finding its way through three centuries of limestone. Footsteps echo. A whisper carries forty feet. The acoustics make every sentence feel like testimony.
atmosphere.crowd: The working group, however many came. The tunnel is wide enough for four abreast. The chamber has room to spare but the darkness makes it feel closer. Everyone gravitates toward the candlelight.
atmosphere.smell: Old stone, cork, tannins, damp earth, beeswax from the candles, the ghost of a thousand vintages breathing through oak barrels stacked floor to ceiling along the tunnel walls

layout.central_chamber: A vaulted limestone room at the end of the main tunnel, carved in a rough oval. A long refectory table of dark walnut, possibly original to the monks, runs the length of the chamber with wooden benches on either side. Five open bottles of wine in a row down the center like a timeline. Someone claimed the head. Someone else drifted to the foot. The distance between them is the length of the table and the width of their disagreement about where to start.
layout.tunnel_north: The main approach, lined with oak barrels and bottle racks disappearing into darkness. Someone walks the tunnel when they need to move, counting barrels, finding patterns in the vintages. The labels go back to 1847. The end of the tunnel has not yet been reached.
layout.tunnel_south: A narrower passage leading to a second, smaller chamber the monks used for prayer. Empty now except for a stone bench and a single candle. When the main table gets too agreeable, someone retreats here. The echo carries any objection back to the group whether they want to hear it or not.
layout.alcove_west: A natural recess in the limestone where water seeps through and mineral deposits have built a shelf over centuries. A geologist's eye could spend an evening here. An overturned crate beside it makes a quiet seat. A barrel nearby serves as a perch. A cork rolls between fingers in the dim light.
layout.tasting_station: A stone ledge near the tunnel entrance with glasses, a decanter, and a corkscrew. Someone stands here pouring measured amounts, insisting that the incremental difference between the 2003 and the 2005 reveals something about how small changes compound. Someone else disagrees about which vintage represents the tail risk. A napkin on the ledge maps the vineyard's distribution network. The 1998 has been opened on the argument that the constraint was nobody being honest enough yet.

objects_of_note: A bottle of 1961 Cheval Blanc on the table, unopened, placed there by the client with a note that reads 'For when you solve it.' Nobody has taken their eyes off it for long.
The problem brief on the table between the third and fourth bottles, stained with a ring from the 2003 Margaux. Nobody wiped it.
A monk's carving in the limestone above the chamber entrance: a single eye inside a triangle. Someone noticed it first. Someone else noticed the noticing.
A row of glasses on the ledge, each with a different pour. One sits untouched at one end. Another is already empty.
A leather-bound guest book on the stone ledge. The previous visitors wrote only initials and dates. The oldest entry is 1923.
mood: Subterranean and honest. The cellar strips away daylight, weather, time, and pretense. Three centuries of monks fermented patience into these walls. The candlelight makes every face look older and more serious than it is. Wine loosens frameworks. The acoustics make it impossible to mutter -- everything said is said to the room. By the third bottle, the models will argue with each other the way the vintages argue on the palate: not to win, but to reveal what the blend could be.
